pattern: Every pair of distinct elements has a product, and any two pairs (with all four indices distinct) that share a product can be arranged into a valid ordered tuple eight different ways, so counting how many pairs collide on each product value gives the full answer directly.
hints: Instead of thinking about four numbers at once, think about pairs: compute the product of every pair of distinct elements and group pairs by their product value.
Any two *different* pairs that happen to have the same product -- and don't share an index -- can be laid out as a valid a*b = c*d tuple.

For a product that C pairs share, the number of ways to pick two of those pairs is C choose 2, and each such choice of two pairs can be arranged into 8 distinct ordered tuples (2 orderings within each pair, times 2 for which pair comes first) -- sum that over every product value.

edge_inputs.description: a product collision hiding among pairs that share a common factor
several distinct pairs all sharing the same product
larger set with multiple independent collisions
values at the upper bound of the value range

stress.description: distinct random values in a narrow-ish range to encourage frequent product collisions
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(4, min(size, 400))
    pool = list(range(1, 2001))
    rng.shuffle(pool)
    return [pool[:n]]
stress.note: narrow value pool relative to n maximises the chance of product collisions while values stay distinct
